lines.text: Midnight. We're {{ELAPSED}} in and the kill count just hit 82 according to Tucker's last report. Wind's picking up outside. Temperature's dropped five degrees since I sat down. This next one's for everybody barricaded in right now who's feeling the walls close in.

Green Day. 'Basket Case.' 1994. Billie Joe Armstrong wrote this about a panic attack he had in the back of a van. The guy's father died when he was ten — fragmented everything. So he turned it into the third biggest single off Dookie.

Dookie cost them three weeks to record. Three weeks. Fantasy Studios in Berkeley, September and October 1993. Sold twenty million copies worldwide. That album changed everything — made it okay for punks to sound like they actually gave a shit about melody.

Rob Cavallo produced it. Same guy who'd go on to produce American Idiot fourteen years later. Cavallo understood something that most people don't: pop-punk isn't a compromise. It's a weapon. You can make people *feel* and still be loud as hell.
Armstrong's talking about wanting to be medicated, wanting to disappear, wanting someone to tell him he's not crazy. You know what? That's the whole Purge right there. Here we go.
NOFX. 'Linoleum.' 1996. Off So Long and Thanks for All the Shoes. Fat Mike and the boys returned to pure speed after Punk in Drublic got so big they didn't know what to do with themselves.

Fat Mike — Michael Burkett — formed NOFX in Los Angeles in 1983 with Erik Sandin and Eric Melvin. They were kids from Fairfax High School who wanted to make noise and say things nobody else was saying. They never signed to a major label. *Never.* That's punk rock as a business strategy.

NOFX released The Decline in 1999 — an eighteen-minute single-track extended play that was basically a musical middle finger to everything they saw falling apart. Fiery. Cynical. Beautiful in how much it hated the world.

By 2003, Fat Mike and the crew were making political records — The War on Errorism. Songs about racism, about American middle-class plight, about how the government was selling lies. They used humor because humor is the only way to survive the truth.
This song right here is just three minutes of 'I don't want to work and I don't want to think about why I don't want to work.' Some nights, that's the most honest thing you can say. Here we go.

The Descendents. 'Hope.' 1982. Off Milo Goes to College. These guys formed in Manhattan Beach, California in 1977 and spent five years becoming the most important band nobody who listens to punk will shut up about.

Milo Aukerman — the vocalist — actually went to college. University of California, San Diego. That's not a bit. That's the album title. The band was always doing weird things that made no sense in the context of 1982 hardcore, and it made *perfect* sense.

They recorded their debut at Media Art studios and released it on their own label, Orca Records, named after Bill Stevenson's fishing boat. Stevenson was the drummer. Guy left Black Flag to rejoin the Descendents because he cared more about his friends than his ego. You see that a lot in punk?

Steven Blush — the American Hardcore guy — said the Descendents made 'cheeky love songs disguised as hardcore blasts.' They sang about girls, about being rejected, about food, about their parents. Topics that made hardcore kids feel less alone.

And here's the thing nobody tells you: they recorded their own farts on 'Orgofart.' Actual farting sounds. The band members cheering each other on in the studio. That's freedom. That's what happens when nobody's watching except the people you trust.

Anti-Flag. 'Die for the Government.' 1996. These guys formed in Pittsburgh in 1988 — Justin Sane and Pat Thetic — and spent the next thirty-five years reminding America that punks don't forgive and don't forget.

Anti-Flag has always been political. Fervent anti-war. Criticism of U.S. foreign policy, corporatism, wealth distribution — all the things that get you labeled a radical if you're not careful. But they didn't care about careful.

In 2001, they teamed up with Fat Mike — our NOFX guy — at Fat Wreck Chords and recorded Underground Network. Howard Zinn wrote essays for the booklet. Political commentary from actual historians. Punk rock as activism as scholarship.

Fast forward to 2003: The Terror State. An album that primarily focused on criticizing the Bush Administration's war on terrorism. Sound familiar? The Purge happened because people stopped listening to bands like this.

Chris No. 2's sister was murdered — killed in violence. Anti-Flag responded by releasing a benefit EP for victims of violent crime. That's what punk rock is *for*. Not aesthetics. Not coolness. Actual solidarity with actual suffering.

tracks.title: Crazy
tracks.artist: Me First and the Gimme Gimmes

lines.text: Me First and the Gimme Gimmes. 'Crazy.' 1997. Off Have a Ball. These guys are a supergroup of cover artists — Spike Slawson, Fat Mike, Joey Cape, and the crew — who formed in San Francisco in 1995 and decided to make a career out of ruining other people's songs in the best possible way.

Have a Ball was 1960s and 1970s pop hits done as punk. Rapid-fire interpretations with a humorous edge. They'd take your grandmother's favorite song and make it sound like it was always meant to be a three-minute adrenaline spike.

Fat Mike got so caught up in one of their covers — 'Wild World' — that he wrote liner notes saying he thought he was Fat Stevens, a guitar-toting Islamic Extremist, and when Spike sang something he mistook it for 'Allah my Lovin'' and condemned him to death for heresy. That's the level of commitment to absurdism.

They played a Bar Mitzvah once. Thirty people. Made a lot of mistakes. Started songs and stopped mid-way through. Released a live album of it. That's confidence — or insanity. In punk rock, they're the same thing.

They wore matching costumes at their shows. Cowboy outfits. Drag. Whatever matched the album. Here's a band that could've been serious and chose to be joyful instead. At midnight on Purge Night, that's a radical act.

Dead Milkmen. 'Bitchin' Camaro.' 1985. Off Big Lizard in My Backyard. Four guys from Philadelphia — Rodney Anonymous, Joe Jack Talcum, Dave Blood, and Dean Clean — who formed the most deliberately stupid punk band in history and made it work.

Joe Genaro started this as a home-recording project in 1979 in Chester County with a friend named Garth. They created an imaginary band with a fictional backstory. Released homemade cassettes. The moniker came from a character in Toni Morrison's Song of Solomon. That's not a joke. That's actual literary depth hiding under layers of absurdism.

Their sound was jangly punk with sardonic humor delivered in thick Philadelphia accents. They sang about toxic waste, jellyfish afterlifes, UFOs, conspiracy theories, and cover bands. None of it made sense and all of it was real.

'Bitchin' Camaro' had an improvised dialogue intro that changed every night. People still come to Dead Milkmen shows specifically to hear how they'll introduce that song. That's the opposite of stadium rock. That's punk rock as a living, breathing, dangerous thing.

Temperature's still dropping. Wind's at maybe eighteen, twenty miles per hour now. If you're listening and you're alone in your apartment right now, these guys are the ones who understood that being weird and being loud is how you survive. Here we go.

tickerItems.text: Madison-wide cumulative: 82 kills as of 00:00. Up 22 from this point last year. East Side continuing to lead.
Temperature holding at 29°F. Northwest winds gusting to 28 mph. Visibility down to 2 miles. Cold front arrived ahead of forecast.
PurgeShield Structural Panels — Last year 1,850 families installed before Purge Night. This year, slots filling fast. Call 608-555-0147.
